title: Healthcheck fails/hangs if containers started through a different storage configuration
body:
### Issue Description

If podman is configured to use a different storage configuration file (e.g. through the `CONTAINERS_STORAGE_CONF` environment variable), any containers with healthchecks will hang with status "starting" because the systemd service for the healthcheck doesn't include the different storage configuration file, so the healthcheck service won't find the container.

The healthcheck service only configures the `PATH` environment variable, so any extra environment variables that change podman's behaviour, such as `CONTAINERS_CONF`, `CONTAINERS_REGISTRIES_CONF`, and `CONTAINERS_STORAGE_CONF` aren't included and can cause healthchecks to fail and/or hang indefinitely.

Running the exact same command in the healthcheck service on the same shell with the environment variables works.

This is a blocking issue for podman-compose with healthchecks.

See the repro for more details.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

```
mkdir -p /tmp/repro
cd /tmp/repro
cat >storage.conf <<EOF
[storage]
driver="overlay"
graphroot="/tmp/repro/podman/storage"
rootless_storage_path="/tmp/repro/podman/storage"
EOF
export CONTAINERS_STORAGE_CONF="/tmp/repro/storage.conf"
container_id=$(podman run -d --health-cmd='true' docker.io/library/httpd)
sleep 10 && podman inspect -f '{{.State.Health}}' $container_id
# Output: {starting 0 []}
timer_unit=$(systemctl --user --type=timer --no-pager --output json list-units | jq -r '.[] | select(.description | contains("healthcheck")) | .unit')
systemctl --user --no-pager status $timer_unit
# Check that it's active and running, trigger is working.
systemctl --user --no-pager status "${timer_unit%.timer}.service"
# Check that it failed.
journalctl --user --no-pager -n 3 -u "${timer_unit%.timer}.service"
# Output: Error: unable to look up 716f7e35b0a44133e83a01eaba2522ea457edc3c79788c32ebfa88291f62724d to perform a health check: no container with name or ID "716f7e35b0a44133e83a01eaba2522ea457edc3c79788c32ebfa88291f62724d" found: no such container
```

### Describe the results you received

Error: unable to look up 716f7e35b0a44133e83a01eaba2522ea457edc3c79788c32ebfa88291f62724d to perform a health check: no container with name or ID "716f7e35b0a44133e83a01eaba2522ea457edc3c79788c32ebfa88291f62724d" found: no such container

### Describe the results you expected

Healthcheck to run successfully.

### podman info output


### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Podman configured to use specific configuration files through `CONTAINERS_CONF`, `CONTAINERS_REGISTRIES_CONF`, and `CONTAINERS_STORAGE_CONF`.

### Additional information

This can be worked around by putting the configuration files in one of the default places that podman expects to find them. However, this "dirties" the system/environment outside a project - if you want to keep everything self-contained as much as possible, you're blocked by this bug.

comments.body:
The storage path issue was already fixed in https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/28498 which is likely good enough for you.

But yes I guess some settings from CONTAINERS_CONF may not be propagated right

> The storage path issue was already fixed in https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/28498 which is likely good enough for you.

I don't think it is, please see my repro - in my real-case scenario I'm also setting the storage path to a completely different place, so it's not using podman's transient storage either. Unless I'm misunderstanding how `--transient-store` works and somehow it captures the actual path for storage that I'm setting.
just look at the commit, it passes all the same --root/--runroot,etc... options now. That is the same options the cleanup process gets.

Thanks, I just built a version of `podman` from commit `3fa89a3c010ce9959e1049b443d27a59e264fe18` and can confirm that the healthchecks are using the correct storage paths.

A small caveat on that: my storage settings initially didn't specify a driver, and I was still using the same storage created with those settings, even after switching the storage driver to "overlay". `podman` worked fine this way when creating the containers, but the healthchecks failed on [this line](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/23cc1a30fbe8234a6de5aa146b1c213cee4d93cb/libpod/sqlite_state.go#L436)  because the healthcheck command explicitly specified "overlay", which was different from the driver "" in the DB.
I don't know whether you folks see this as something to be fixed, but based on the description of the "driver" setting in the docs ("This field is required to guarantee proper operation."), I'm taking it as something that doesn't need to be looked at. I cleaned up the entire storage directory and recreated things, and the healthchecks are working now.

I'm guessing some other settings that aren't being propagated might still be needed for proper healthcheck functionality, but you folks are obviously way more knowledgeable about that, so feel free to rename/reword the issue and keep it open if so. Otherwise, my issue is fixed - storage settings seem to be propagated correctly with that PR you linked, so if nothing else still needs to be propagated, this issue can be closed.

>  I don't know whether you folks see this as something to be fixed, but based on the description of the "driver" setting in the docs ("This field is required to guarantee proper operation."), I'm taking it as something that doesn't need to be looked at. I cleaned up the entire storage directory and recreated things, and the healthchecks are working now.

Huh, there was like a big warning if the "driver"  field was not set in storage.conf so did you ignore it? However this is relevant I reworked all config files and made driver optional, it should not throw db validation error in that case so this is something we need to look at.

cc @mheon

> Huh, there was like a big warning if the "driver" field was not set in storage.conf so did you ignore it?

I initially did not set a driver in `storage.conf` and got the warning after running a `podman pull` command (if I remember correctly), and eventually I set a driver in the config, but the storage had already been populated with what I'm assuming is the `""` driver (from the error message that I got), and that's what caused the healthcheck to fail when it was given an explicit `"overlay"` driver.

Also keep in mind the commit I used to build a local version of `podman` - it's ~1 month old. I tried building with a much newer commit, but it failed on a `network create` command, so I decided to just grab the earliest commit that seemed to pass the most tests from the `main` branch and still included the changes from the linked PR.

We have normalization for a lot of things in the DB to detect `""` and replace it with the default for that field, but it doesn't look like we ever did that for the database. It's an easy fix, but at some point we need to rework this logic entirely. Partially to allow changing parameters when there are no containers/pods/volumes present, and partially to figure out how the empty string is getting in the database and stop it from happening. Could be part of the larger refactoring of runtime/database init I wanted to do in 6.1 to fix the absolute mess that I made with Bolt to SQLite migration in 6.0
Ever did that for the *driver* I mean
